facebook.content_angle: Early Vertical Forearm (high elbow catch) — the most under-drilled freestyle technique in club swimming and the fix that adds real distance per stroke
facebook.post_text:
🔥 This one freestyle fix is worth more seconds than almost any set you'll swim this week.

If you've ever trained hard and still felt slow — your catch is quietly the culprit. Most club swimmers enter the water and immediately pull with the shoulder. That's not a catch. That's a push — and it leaks the majority of your power before the stroke even starts.

Here's what elite swimmers do differently:
👉 At entry, keep your **elbow high and wide** — fingertips below wrist, wrist below elbow
👉 This is the Early Vertical Forearm: your forearm faces *backward* before the pull begins
👉 Now instead of pulling with your shoulder, you're pressing a full forearm-paddle against the water using your lats and back

The moment you feel a real catch, you'll wonder how you ever swam without it. 😳

Here's why this one fix changes everything:
✔️ EVF engages your lats and upper back — your most powerful pulling muscles, not your shoulder
✔️ **A proper catch adds 4–8cm of distance per stroke** — across a 200m freestyle, that's real seconds back
✔️ Jaked's compression fabric locks your body position flat in the water, so every catch translates forward — not into wasted rotation

Have you ever had a coach fix your catch live in one session? 👇
facebook.engagement_bait: Drop a 🙌 if your coach has ever pulled you out of the lane to fix your catch — or tag the swimmer who desperately needs this drill! 👇
